lessons.title: std::bit_cast, std::source_location, std::numbers
lessons.motivation: Before C++20, the only way to inspect a value's bit representation was a combination of `reinterpret_cast` or `memcpy` — the former is formally undefined behaviour due to strict aliasing rules, while the latter does not work in `constexpr` contexts. The macros `__FILE__`, `__LINE__`, and `__FUNCTION__` provided diagnostic information but were rigidly tied to the call site, making logging helpers awkward. Mathematical constants (π, e, √2) were defined by each programmer individually, with the potential for inaccurate values. C++20 solves all three problems: `std::bit_cast` is a type-safe, `constexpr`-compatible replacement for the `memcpy` trick; `std::source_location` replaces diagnostic macros and is passed as a default parameter; `std::numbers` provides canonical constants at maximum precision for any floating-point type.
lessons.theory:
## std::bit_cast

`std::bit_cast<To>(from)` (P0476) reinterprets the bytes of an object of type `From` as an object of type `To` — without undefined behaviour:

```cpp
#include <bit>

float f = 1.0f;
uint32_t bits = std::bit_cast<uint32_t>(f);  // OK — correct and constexpr
```

Requirements:
- `sizeof(To) == sizeof(From)` — sizes must match (checked at compile time).
- Both types must be *trivially copyable*.

Before C++20, this was done with `memcpy` inside a helper function. `bit_cast` achieves the same result but works in a `constexpr` context and needs no intermediate buffer:

```cpp
constexpr uint32_t pi_bits = std::bit_cast<uint32_t>(3.14159265f);  // works in constexpr
```

**Typical use** — working with IEEE 754 bit fields:
- Sign bit of a float — bit 31.
- Exponent — bits 30–23.
- Mantissa — bits 22–0.

`bit_cast` allows reading and manipulating these fields without UB.

## std::source_location

`std::source_location` (P1208) stores information about a location in source code: file name, line number, column number, and function name. The primary usage pattern is as a default parameter in logging functions:

```cpp
#include <source_location>

void log(std::string_view msg,
         std::source_location loc = std::source_location::current()) {
    std::cout << loc.file_name() << ":" << loc.line() << " " << msg << "\n";
}

log("hello");  // captures the file and line of the log() call, not the function body
```

Methods:
- `loc.file_name()` — path to the file (C string).
- `loc.line()` — line number (`uint_least32_t`).
- `loc.column()` — column number.
- `loc.function_name()` — name of the enclosing function.

Key detail: `std::source_location::current()` as a default argument captures the location of the *call site*, not the function definition — that is exactly what makes the pattern useful for logging.

## std::numbers

`<numbers>` (P0631) introduces mathematical constants in the `std::numbers` namespace:

| Constant | Value |
|----------|-------|
| `std::numbers::pi` | π |
| `std::numbers::e` | e (base of the natural logarithm) |
| `std::numbers::sqrt2` | √2 |
| `std::numbers::phi` | φ (golden ratio) |
| `std::numbers::log2e` | log₂(e) |
| `std::numbers::log10e` | log₁₀(e) |
| `std::numbers::ln2` | ln(2) |
| `std::numbers::ln10` | ln(10) |
| `std::numbers::inv_pi` | 1/π |
| `std::numbers::inv_sqrt3` | 1/√3 |
| `std::numbers::egamma` | γ (Euler–Mascheroni constant) |

Constants default to `double`. For other floating-point types, use the template variant `_v<T>`:

```cpp
double pi_d = std::numbers::pi;                      // double
float pi_f = std::numbers::pi_v<float>;              // float
long double pi_ld = std::numbers::pi_v<long double>; // maximum precision
```

examples.title: bit_cast: inspecting float bits without UB
examples.expectedOutput:
1.0f bits: 0x3F800000
reconstructed: -1
pi bits: 0x40490FDB
is -3.5 negative: 1
is  3.5 negative: 0

examples.title: source_location as a default parameter and std::numbers
examples.expectedOutput:
[/app/example.cpp:15] program started
[/app/example.cpp:16] computing area
pi  = 3.1415926536
e   = 2.7182818285
sqrt2 = 1.4142135624
circle area r=5: 78.5398
pi (long double) = 3.141592653589793

lessons.title: std::midpoint, std::lerp, std::erase_if, std::to_address
lessons.motivation: Several classic C++ operations had hidden traps: `(a + b) / 2` for computing an average overflows for large `int` values; manual linear interpolation does not guarantee exact boundary values and can produce results outside [a, b]; the erase-remove idiom `v.erase(std::remove_if(...), v.end())` is verbose and easy to get wrong; obtaining a raw pointer from an arbitrary pointer-like type (smart pointer, iterator) without depending on the concrete type was not possible in generic code. C++20 addresses each problem with a dedicated utility.
lessons.theory:
## std::midpoint

`std::midpoint(a, b)` (P0811, `<numeric>`) computes the midpoint of two values without overflow:

```cpp
int32_t a = 2000000000, b = 2000000001;
int32_t mid = std::midpoint(a, b);  // 2000000000 — correct
// Classic bug: (a + b) / 2 — signed integer overflow (UB)
```

Works with integers, pointers, and floating-point types. For pointers, `midpoint(p, q)` returns a pointer to the midpoint of the range `[p, q)` (requires both pointers to point into the same array).

For `float`/`double`, the result is accurate and never goes outside the range `[a, b]`.

## std::lerp

`std::lerp(a, b, t)` (P0811, `<cmath>`) performs linear interpolation: `a + t*(b - a)`.

Standard guarantees:
- When `t == 0`, the result is exactly `a`.
- When `t == 1`, the result is exactly `b`.
- When `a == b`, the result equals `a` for any `t` (no NaN).
- When `t` is outside `[0, 1]`, the result may go beyond `[a, b]` — this is extrapolation, not an error.

```cpp
std::lerp(0.0, 10.0, 0.5)  // 5.0
std::lerp(0.0, 10.0, 0.0)  // exactly 0.0
std::lerp(0.0, 10.0, 1.0)  // exactly 10.0
```

## std::erase and std::erase_if

`std::erase(container, value)` / `std::erase_if(container, pred)` (P1209, `<vector>`, `<list>`, `<map>`, …) are a unified replacement for the erase-remove idiom:

```cpp
// Before C++20 (erase-remove idiom):
v.erase(std::remove_if(v.begin(), v.end(), pred), v.end());

// C++20:
std::erase_if(v, pred);
```

`std::erase_if` returns the number of elements removed. Works for all standard containers — `vector`, `list`, `deque`, `map`, `set`, `unordered_map`, `unordered_set`.

`std::erase(container, value)` removes all elements equal to `value` (equivalent to `erase_if` with predicate `== value`).

## std::to_address

`std::to_address(ptr)` (P0653, `<memory>`) obtains a raw pointer from any type satisfying the `Pointer` concept:

```cpp
auto up = std::make_unique<int>(42);
int* raw = std::to_address(up);   // same as up.get()

std::vector<int> v{1, 2, 3};
int* p = std::to_address(v.begin());  // raw pointer to the first element
```

The main use case is generic code that works with any `Pointer` type (smart pointers, random-access iterators, fancy pointers in allocators). Without `to_address`, there is no uniform way to get a raw address from all these types.

## std::assume_aligned

`std::assume_aligned<N>(ptr)` (P1007, `<memory>`) is a compiler hint about pointer alignment. It does not check alignment at runtime — violating the guarantee leads to UB:

```cpp
void process(float* data, size_t n) {
    float* aligned = std::assume_aligned<64>(data);  // SIMD-friendly
    // compiler is allowed to use aligned load instructions
    for (size_t i = 0; i < n; ++i) aligned[i] *= 2.0f;
}
```

examples.title: midpoint and lerp: safe average and interpolation
erase_if and to_address: element removal and pointer access
examples.expectedOutput:
after erase even: 1 3 5 7 
after erase 3: 1 2 4 
long words: hello world 
via to_address: 42
first element: 10

lessons.title: String/Container Improvements and Other Utilities
lessons.motivation: C++20 brought a number of small but long-awaited improvements to the standard library: checking a string's prefix or suffix no longer requires comparisons with `substr` or `rfind`; looking up a key in a `map` or `set` without constructing a temporary is now a direct method rather than the roundabout `find() != end()`; stripping `const`, `volatile`, and a reference from a type required chaining three trait templates, now it takes one; distinguishing `T[N]` (a bounded array) from `T[]` (an unbounded array) is finally possible with standard traits. Each of these small things adds up, making everyday C++ noticeably cleaner.
lessons.theory:
## starts_with / ends_with for std::string and std::string_view

`std::basic_string::starts_with(x)` and `ends_with(x)` (P0457) check whether a string begins or ends with `x`. `x` may be a `string_view`, a single `char`, or a C-string:

```cpp
std::string s = "https://example.com";
s.starts_with("https")   // true
s.ends_with(".com")      // true
s.ends_with('m')         // true (char overload)
```

Both methods are also available on `std::string_view`.

## contains for map, set, and unordered containers

Before C++20, checking for key presence meant: `m.find(key) != m.end()`. C++20 adds `contains(key)`:

```cpp
std::map<std::string, int> m{...};
m.contains("key")   // true/false, without an iterator pair
```

Works for `map`, `set`, `multimap`, `multiset`, `unordered_map`, `unordered_set`, and their multi variants.

## std::remove_cvref_t

`std::remove_cvref_t<T>` (P0550, `<type_traits>`) removes `const`, `volatile`, and a reference in one step. Equivalent to `std::remove_cv_t<std::remove_reference_t<T>>`:

```cpp
std::remove_cvref_t<const int&>     // int
std::remove_cvref_t<volatile int&&> // int
std::remove_cvref_t<int* const>     // int* (pointer is not stripped)
```

Particularly useful in template code dealing with forwarding references.

## is_bounded_array and is_unbounded_array

`std::is_bounded_array_v<T>` (P1357) returns `true` for `T[N]` (an array of known size).
`std::is_unbounded_array_v<T>` returns `true` for `T[]` (an array of unknown size).

```cpp
std::is_bounded_array_v<int[5]>   // true
std::is_unbounded_array_v<int[]>  // true
std::is_bounded_array_v<int>      // false
```

Used internally by `std::make_unique` and `std::make_shared` to choose between `make_unique<T[]>(n)` and `make_unique<T>(args...)`.

## std::identity

`std::identity` (P0777, `<functional>`) is an identity functor: `std::identity{}(x)` returns `x` unchanged. It serves as the default projection parameter in `std::ranges` algorithms:

```cpp
// Explicit identity — same as the default:
std::ranges::sort(v, std::less{}, std::identity{});
// Equivalent to:
std::ranges::sort(v);

// Useful when writing generic algorithms that accept a projection:
template<typename Proj = std::identity>
void my_algo(auto& range, Proj proj = {});
```

## std::make_shared_for_overwrite and make_unique_for_overwrite

`std::make_shared_for_overwrite<T>()` (P1020) creates an object without default-initialization — bypassing the zero-initialization that `make_shared` performs for trivial types. For arrays, `make_shared_for_overwrite<T[]>(n)` leaves elements uninitialized. Use this when you are about to overwrite every byte immediately:

```cpp
auto buf = std::make_shared_for_overwrite<int[]>(1024);
// buf[i] contains indeterminate values — must write before reading
std::fill_n(buf.get(), 1024, 0);
```

## operator<=> for containers

C++20 added `operator<=>` (three-way comparison) for all standard containers. `std::vector`, `std::list`, `std::map`, and others can now be compared lexicographically via `<=>` instead of explicit calls to `std::lexicographical_compare`.

examples.title: starts_with/ends_with, map::contains, set::contains
examples.expectedOutput:
starts with https: true
ends with v2:      true
starts with http:  true
ends with .html:   false
ends with /v2: true
has timeout: true
has debug:   false
7 is prime: true
9 is prime: false

examples.title: remove_cvref_t, is_bounded_array, std::identity, make_shared_for_overwrite
examples.expectedOutput:
remove_cvref_t<const int&> == int: true
remove_cvref_t<volatile int&&> == int: true
int[5] bounded: true
int[]  unbounded: true
int    bounded: false
sorted: 1 1 2 3 4 5 6 9 
squares: 0 1 4 9 16
